text: For endurance exercise lasting 30 minutes or more, the most likely contributors to fatigue are dehydration and carbohydrate depletion.
Gastrointestinal problems, hyperthermia, and hyponatraemia can reduce performance and become health-threatening in events lasting more than 4 hours.
High muscle glycogen concentrations at the start of endurance exercise are beneficial, but do not necessarily require the traditional supercompensation (carb-loading) protocol to achieve.
Carbohydrate delivery to working muscles should be individualized based on both absolute exercise intensity and event duration.
Endurance athletes should limit body mass losses through sweating to 2-3% of body mass to minimize the performance impact of dehydration.
Gastrointestinal problems during endurance events may be triggered by highly concentrated carbohydrate solutions, hyperosmotic drinks, and intake of fibre, fat, and protein.
Hyponatraemia risk is elevated among slower competitors with very high intakes of water or other low-sodium drinks during endurance events.
